services.title: 3 clear plans.
services.description: Fully hand-coded website, never generated by a no-code tool like Wix or WordPress. No lifetime subscription, no interchangeable template.

configureCard.title: Configure your project, get your quote
configureCard.description: Pick your plan, check the options you need, get a detailed estimate by email. No payment at this stage.

q1.question: Why not a no-code tool like Wix, Squarespace or Webflow?
q1.p1: With a no-code tool, you get started fast. But you quickly lose control: design limited by generic templates (used on thousands of other sites), lifetime monthly subscription, limited performance and SEO, zero control over what happens under the hood.
q1.p2: What I offer is <accent>a website coded from scratch, that you own 100%</accent>. You control every aspect: a unique design tailored to your activity, SEO, performance, fluidity, speed, and the choice of techniques used to evolve your site over time.

q2.question: How long until I have my website?
q2.p1: <accent>5 business days</accent> for Essential, <accent>10 days</accent> for Standard, <accent>3 weeks</accent> for Premium. The timeline starts once I receive your materials (copy, photos, logo).

q3.question: Do you create mockups before coding?
q3.p1: Today, in most cases: <accent>no, and it's intentional</accent>. We agree on a visual direction together (palette, typography, mood, references you like and dislike), then I move straight to code.
q3.p2: You see your site evolve <accent>for real, not as a frozen mockup</accent>. It's faster, the result is necessarily faithful to the final product, and every tweak goes through the revision hours included in your plan.
q3.p3: On the most ambitious Premium projects, I can offer mini-mockups or pair with a designer partner. We discuss this on a case-by-case basis.

q4.question: How does the collaboration actually work?
q4.p1: We start with a <accent>free 15-min video call</accent> to understand your activity and your world. If we work together, I send you a visual brief to fill out (colors, references, mood), then a <accent>moodboard to validate by email</accent>.
q4.p2: Once the direction is locked, I code the first page and send it as a preview. You validate this first draft, I code the rest in the same direction. Adjustments go through the revision hours included in your plan.

q5.question: Can I see examples of websites you've built?
q5.p1: Yes: a selection is on the <projets>projects</projets> page, and 3 case studies are highlighted directly on each plan page.
q5.p2: From an artist's e-commerce site selling internationally to apps deployed on hundreds of tablets in TotalEnergies stations. Each project has its own identity.

q6.question: What if I want an option that isn't in my plan?
q6.p1: Each plan has a clear scope. If you want an option that isn't included (for example a 100% bespoke design in Essential), you need to move up to the higher plan. <accent>No à la carte mixing</accent> beyond the listed options: it keeps deadlines short and pricing clear.

q7.question: What if I want to update my site after launch?
q7.p1: <accent>2h of revisions</accent> included in Essential, <accent>5h</accent> in Standard, <accent>10h</accent> in Premium. These hours cover content adjustments, small visual tweaks, color variants on specific blocks, etc.
q7.p2: Beyond that: <accent>€60/h</accent> with a quote before intervention. Or monthly maintenance at <accent>€50/month</accent> (1h of edits included + technical follow-up).

q8.question: I don't have a domain name. What should I do?
q8.p1: I'll help you buy your domain in your own name. You own it, you keep control. The cost (~€10 to €15/year) is on you.

meta.title: About
meta.description: Front-end developer specialized in React & Next.js, based in Île-de-France. Discover my journey.

job.title: Front-End Developer
job.description: The foundation of my work since 2022. I started in a startup at <qare>Qare</qare> (telemedicine), then joined <artefact>Artefact</artefact>, one of Europe's largest consulting firms in data and AI, first on the agency side then on the consulting side. Concrete expertise in demanding professional environments.

parallel.title: Website builder
parallel.description: The other side of my activity, driven by my passion for development and design. I help SMBs, freelancers and individuals build their online presence. My own business, where I handle everything from A to Z and put my personal touch on every project.

story.title: From startup
story.p1: I made my tech debut at <accent>Qare</accent>. A major telemedicine player in France, in full post-Covid growth, where I discovered agile methodology in a startup, working alongside product teams (Product Owners, Product Managers, designers), and the demand of shipping concrete features iterated continuously on a product used every day by thousands of patients.
story.p2:
I then joined Artefact. One of Europe's largest consulting firms specialized in data and AI. Now valued at over one billion, it's a true French unicorn working with the world's biggest brands. I was lucky to step into that world.

I first joined Artefact 3000, the group's ad agency, in its creative studio — total creative freedom, where ideas constantly flow. Then in 2025, I moved to the consulting side, into a more structured environment (consultants, data analysts, data engineers), to explore different kinds of projects.
story.p3: Thanks to Artefact, I had the opportunity to work with top-tier brands: <argedis>TotalEnergies</argedis> (app deployed on hundreds of tablets at service stations), <sweetime>Paris Airports</sweetime> (digital contest reaching hundreds of thousands of travelers), <bnp>BNP Paribas</bnp> (generative AI e-learning module deployed internally), Robertet (perfumery). And beyond my own projects, I got to see other ambitious work being built around me for major brands, learning a huge amount from this stimulating ecosystem.
story.p4: My DNA: <bold>front-end</bold>. Even though today I'm increasingly leaning toward full-stack. What drives me above all is the interface: the visual rendering, the user experience, the perceived quality.
story.p5: Alongside my full-time job, I also help <bold>SMBs, freelancers and individuals</bold> create their websites. An activity I built out of passion for code and design, where I can handle every project from A to Z, with no intermediary. I notably built <bald>Bald</bald>, a custom e-commerce site for a painter who sells internationally. And more are on the way.
story.p6: What motivates me daily: finding clean technical solutions, imagining unique interfaces, chasing quality (clean code, best practices, design details). Whether for a major brand or a freelance client, my goal is the same: deliver a product that exceeds expectations, and make sure trust is always well placed.

artefact.description: Moved to the consulting side to explore different kinds of projects and join the world of consultants, data analysts and data engineers. I now cover the full-stack scope via Next.js, in collaboration with the creative studio teams.

artefact3000.description: Creative studio nested inside Artefact 3000 (the ad agency of the Artefact group). Total creative freedom, demanding web projects for major brands: TotalEnergies (Argedis), Paris Airports (Extime), Robertet, BNP Paribas. Tablet apps, large-scale digital contests, interactive e-learning modules.

bnp-paribas-elearning.description: Gamified e-learning module deployed internally at BNP Paribas — 6 chapters, 5 types of mini-games, 6 AI-generated videos, a guide character, SCORM 1.2 compliant.
bnp-paribas-elearning.descriptionPublic: The **introduction screen**: __AI-nstein__ — an AI character inspired by Einstein — guides the user through a __chat interface__, sending messages and asking questions answered by clicking. Alongside it, the __main course module__ displays the interactive content for the current chapter. At the bottom, a __progress bar__ tracks advancement across all six chapters. The interactive games come in the following chapters.
bnp-paribas-elearning.intro: One of the projects that challenged me most. Built at Artefact 3000 for **BNP Paribas**, this gamified e-learning module trains thousands of employees in the fundamentals of generative AI. **6 chapters, 5 types of mini-games, 6 AI-generated videos**, a guide character. And above all, a bet few agencies dare to make: **replacing Articulate Storyline with a full React app**, packaged as SCORM 1.2 to plug into any enterprise LMS.

sliderSets.title: The interactive mini-games
sliderSets.description: Five distinct mini-game types, built from scratch with PixiJS. DataShield: intercept sensitive data in free fall. A prompt categorizer via drag-and-drop. A hallucination hunt. And for chapter 5, the press convoy: an invisible physical object represents the case on the conveyor belt, synced frame-by-frame with the video, to trigger entry into the presses at the right moment.

sliderSets.title: Learning, recaps & results
sliderSets.description: Each chapter follows the same rhythm: an AI-generated intro video, explanations, an interactive game, then a recap or results screen that clearly separates right from wrong. At the end of the journey, a skills passport validates all chapters.

technologies.detail: BNP's LMS only accepts static applications packaged as SCORM — no server, no external API. React + Vite produces a self-contained build that any LMS can read, whereas Next.js requires a Node.js server.

The e-learning standard from the 2000s, used by enterprise LMSes. A SCORM package is a ZIP archive with an XML manifest describing the module, and a JavaScript API the LMS exposes to the page. `pipwerks-scorm-api-wrapper` abstracts LMS calls (`LMSSetValue`, `LMSCommit`, etc.) to send score, progress, and completion from React back to BNP's LMS.

The mini-games (brick game, data shield, hallucination detection) required high-performance canvas rendering. PixiJS provides a WebGL engine with Canvas 2D fallback, perfectly suited to complex animations of moving objects.

Used for drag-and-drop games: source ranking, concept matching. The library handles accessibility props (ARIA, keyboard navigation) natively, which streamlined the full accessibility mode implementation.
GSAP drives orchestrated transitions (chapter entrances, synchronized text/image/sound timelines). Framer Motion handles React component animations (chat bubble appearances, quiz card transitions).

The UI had dozens of specific components: chat bubbles, quiz cards, circular progress bars, results screens. Storybook allowed validating each visually and functionally in isolation, before wiring them into the state machine.

challenges.titre: Replacing Articulate Storyline with React within the agency
challenges.solution: Artefact's SCORM modules were produced with Articulate Storyline — a no-code tool, fast, but design-constrained, animations impossible, no custom interactivity. Freelancers were paid to produce slides. The goal: prove we could deliver the same SCORM 1.2 compatibility with a real React app — and change the agency's workflow for all future e-learning clients. The technique exists, but it's rarely implemented in an agency context where Articulate is the norm.

challenges.titre: Understanding and implementing the SCORM 1.2 protocol
challenges.solution: SCORM 1.2 is a standard from the 2000s. A SCORM package is a ZIP archive with an XML manifest (`imsmanifest.xml`) describing the module, and a JavaScript API (`LMSInitialize`, `LMSSetValue`, `LMSCommit`, etc.) that the LMS exposes to the page. I studied the spec, understood the communication protocol, then integrated `pipwerks-scorm-api-wrapper` to abstract LMS calls — enabling score, progress, and completion status to flow from the React app back to BNP's LMS.

challenges.titre: State machine for a complex pedagogical flow
challenges.solution: 6 chapters, an intro, a final sequence, dozens of conditional steps based on user responses — without any server-side routing. The entire flow is modeled through Zustand state machine stores. Each step exposes its entry and exit conditions, its contribution to progress, and its conditional rendering.

challenges.titre: Canvas mini-games in a static SPA
challenges.solution: Each chapter has its own mini-game — a brick game with paddle and ball (PixiJS), a shield intercepting falling data, an AI hallucination hunt, a package categorizer. Integrating PixiJS into React required manually managing the canvas scene lifecycle to prevent memory leaks between chapter transitions.

challenges.titre: Full accessibility — keyboard navigation, audio description, zoom
challenges.solution: BNP required strict accessibility compliance. When the module opens, the user selects their language, enables or disables audio description, sets the zoom level, and chooses between standard navigation and keyboard navigation (Tab). In keyboard mode, every focusable element is announced by voice before interaction — the user hears what they're positioned on before pressing Enter to confirm. We even pushed to implement keyboard drag & drop, before disabling it in production to avoid overloading the experience. This was probably the longest and most tedious part of the project — every component, every game, every transition had to be tested with tabindex, aria attributes, and voice announcements.

LMS communication · score · progress · completion
High-performance WebGL canvas mini-games
Accessible drag & drop · ARIA · keyboard navigation
Orchestrated text · image · sound timelines
React UI component animations
Pedagogical flow state machine (6 chapters)
Visual component validation in isolation

argedis-totalenergies.description: Touch application deployed on hundreds of tablets in TotalEnergies stations to showcase local partner producers — continuously managed and updated.
argedis-totalenergies.descriptionPublic:
**An interface built for everyone.** Simple, fluid, immediate. Station visitors instantly understand who the nearby local producers are, where they're located, and what they make.

**Each region has its own world.** The map shifts with the territory. Colors change. Icons change. Every region has its own visual identity — a serious design effort to root the user in their land.

**Producer to producer.** The experience is built for browsing: one tap, and you're onto the next. Simple, accessible, frictionless — even for users less comfortable with technology.
argedis-totalenergies.descriptionTech: Fully static Next.js 14 App Router architecture. Every [region]/[locale]/[station]/[producer] route is pre-generated at build time via generateStaticParams — the app works offline once the APK is installed on the tablet. Content is managed via Contentful and automatically refreshed every 24 hours via a server-triggered rebuild. Application packaged as an Android APK via @ducanh2912/next-pwa to work around installation limitations on proprietary station tablets. Available in French and English via Next.js native i18n routing.
argedis-totalenergies.intro: If you stop at a TotalEnergies station, you'll likely spot screens in the local products section — I was in charge of building this application. Developed at Artefact 3000 for Argedis, a TotalEnergies subsidiary, it's a **nationwide application** showcasing local partner producers, deployed on hundreds of tablets across France.
imageCaptions:
Each region has its own world. The main map image is an integrated Lottie — an animated background with local cultural references — that changes entirely from one region to the next: **Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur** in golden tones, **Bourgogne-Franche-Comté** in deep purples, **Bretagne** in ocean blues.

A radial gradient dynamically adapts the background to the Lottie's dominant color. On the map, each producer is positioned at their exact location on the territory, connected to the TotalEnergies logo by dotted lines. An animated hand invites users to browse the producers clockwise.

On tapping a portrait, the map fades out and gives way to the full profile: photo, story, products, distance from the station. **Previous** and **next** always within reach.

Navigating between producers combines a crossfade on the photo (Framer Motion) and a fluid animation on the container height, which adjusts to the length of each producer's text.

The **FR / EN** toggle doesn't reload the page — both versions are loaded when the app opens.

technologies.detail: One dynamic route per region (/[region]), all pages pre-generated at build time via generateStaticParams. Station, producer, and language selection is entirely handled client-side — no URL change.
Manages all content: text, photos, Lottie files, and coordinates for each producer. Editable by Argedis teams without developer involvement. FR and EN data are loaded simultaneously at launch.
Used for transitions between producers (photo crossfade + animated container height via useRef), the main map → producer card transition, and appearance/disappearance animations via AnimatePresence.
The app is packaged as an APK for installation on station Android tablets. Thanks to full static generation, it runs entirely offline. Content updates automatically via a scheduled rebuild.

challenges.titre: Main map → producer card transition
challenges.solution: On tapping a producer, the large Lottie map shrinks and moves to exactly match the producer card's position, then fades out. The producer card appears at the same instant with a slight scale and coordinated delay via Framer Motion — so both cards are never visible simultaneously and the transition feels continuous.

challenges.titre: Navigation animations between producers
challenges.solution: Two simultaneous effects: a photo crossfade (the next photo rises in opacity via Framer Motion as the current one fades) and a height animation on the text container. Since `height: auto` can't be animated natively, a useRef measures the actual content height on each change and updates a state in pixels — the CSS transition then takes over.

challenges.titre: Pixel-perfect positioning across hundreds of cards
challenges.solution: Each producer is positioned via x/y coordinates stored in Contentful. The system adapts to all tablet resolutions for accurate rendering on every regional map.

challenges.titre: PWA → Android APK
challenges.solution: PWAs are no longer accepted on Android — and soon won't be on iOS either. Installing the app as a simple web app was no longer possible. The application had to be converted to a native APK via a wrapper, while ensuring the behavior remained identical — especially offline mode.

challenges.titre: Full static generation (generateStaticParams)
challenges.solution: All regional pages are pre-generated at build time via generateStaticParams — the App Router equivalent of the old getStaticProps / getStaticPaths. This is also what makes the APK viable offline: all data, images, and content are bundled at build time. Producer switching, FR/EN navigation — everything is instant, without any network call.

challenges.titre: Language switching without routing
challenges.solution: FR/EN switching doesn't go through the URL. Both versions of the Contentful data are loaded simultaneously at initialization. A simple client state toggles between them — invisible to the user, instant on screen.

challenges.titre: Maintenance at the scale of hundreds of producers
challenges.solution: The Contentful architecture lets non-developer teams manage all content — adding producers, updating text, photos, Lottie files — without any technical intervention.

pointsCles.label: Stakes
items: Hundreds of tablets deployed in TotalEnergies stations across France
Hundreds of stations, and even more registered producers

pointsCles.label: Challenges
items: Offline operation — the app is packaged as an Android APK, no connection required
Content updated regularly, without technical intervention

pointsCles.label: Highlights
items: 100% touch interface, available in French and English
Each region has its own visual identity

argedis-totalenergies.resultats: Hundreds of TotalEnergies stations equipped across France. Local producers have a permanent digital showcase, visible to millions of travelers. A living product, maintained and enriched continuously since 2024.

sweetime-adp-extime.description: Digital contest for Paris Airports travelers: a virtual scratch card to win discounts in Extime stores.
sweetime-adp-extime.descriptionPublic:
**One ticket, one shot.** The traveler lands on the welcome screen, invited to try their luck. They enter their email — the card flips to reveal the scratch zone. Everything is designed to be played in a few seconds, standing, before boarding.

**Scratch to reveal.** The reward is hidden under a touch surface drawn on canvas. The player scratches with their finger — and the prize appears automatically: **lollipop**, **-5% discount**, or **-20% discount**.

**An experience in six steps.** Welcome → email entry → card flip → scratching → winning result or better luck next time screen. Every transition is animated, every step flows without reloading — a seamless experience from start to finish.
sweetime-adp-extime.intro: For Extime, the luxury travel retail brand of Paris Airports, I built a digital contest that reached **hundreds of thousands of users** in just a few weeks. Travelers received a **physical voucher with QR code** at purchase in store — by scanning it, they accessed the game and could scratch a virtual ticket to win a **reward**: **lollipop** or **discount code** valid in airport stores.

sliderSets.title: The full journey
sliderSets.description: In a matter of seconds, the traveler enters their email, flips the card, and scratches to reveal their prize. Each step flows without reloading — from sign-up to discount voucher, everything is smooth and designed to be played standing, before boarding.

technologies.detail: Client-side interface, but MongoDB calls can't go directly through the browser — a critical security flaw on an operation of this scale. Next.js API routes serve as a secure server proxy, without deploying a separate backend.

Short operation, intense write load, flexible data structure by store. MongoDB absorbs thousands of simultaneous calls without degradation, where a relational database would have required more complex concurrency management.
Touch scratching is impossible to replicate with standard HTML/CSS. Canvas allows precise control over drawing and progressive erasure to reveal the reward under the finger.
Many graphical elements to build before the funnel (card states, animations, decorative frame). Storybook allowed validating each component in isolation without navigating the whole flow.
Two types of animations coexist: pre-rendered design sequences (candy rain) via Lottie, and programmatic transitions (decorations, appearances) via Framer Motion.
Instant FR/EN switching client-side without URL change — the terminal is identified via UTM parameters in the URL, not via routing.

challenges.titre: Canvas scratch card and responsive frame
challenges.solution: Touch scratching relies entirely on Canvas — the only way to progressively erase a drawn surface under a finger. The stroke must stay smooth even at high speed, and the reveal triggers automatically once a threshold of scratched surface is reached. The decorative frame (candy style) is also drawn on canvas: its dimensions dynamically adapt to the container to remain proportional on all screen formats, from phone to tablet.

challenges.titre: State machine funnel and card flip
challenges.solution: The experience follows 6 sequential steps (welcome, email entry, flip, scratching, win, better luck). The reward is assigned server-side before the card flips — the player can't know in advance what they'll win. Every transition is animated to keep the funnel flowing without reloading.

challenges.titre: Anti-fraud: physical QR code and double verification
challenges.solution: Access requires a QR code handed out physically in store — without it, playing is impossible. A first client-side check blocks repeated attempts from the same device. If this barrier is bypassed, a server verification via email takes over. The person can't play again, whether they won or not.

challenges.titre: Probabilistic distribution with limited stock
challenges.solution: Three rewards with different probabilities. If a prize tier is drawn but its stock is exhausted, the player gets 'Better luck next time' — no bounce to another reward. This guarantees a strictly controlled distribution throughout the operation.

challenges.titre: Animations: rotating background, Lottie and Framer Motion
challenges.solution: The background continuously rotates at two speeds depending on the step. The candy rain on winning is a Lottie sequence provided by design, played in a single pass. The candy decorations progressively appear as soon as the user leaves the main screen.

pointsCles.label: Stakes
items: 1 to 2-week operation across all Roissy terminals — hundreds of thousands of users in very little time
Critical availability: no interruption tolerated during the contest

pointsCles.label: Challenges
items: Double-layer anti-fraud: client cookie per terminal + MongoDB email verification
Probabilistic distribution with limited stock — if a prize tier is exhausted, the draw lands on 'Better luck', no bounce to another prize

pointsCles.label: Highlights
items: Full gamified experience: card flip, touch scratching, Lottie candy rain, and QR code capture
Available in French and English, no account creation required

sweetime-adp-extime.resultats: The operation attracted several hundred thousand participants over one to two weeks, with an engagement rate above expectations. The game contributed to increased traffic in Extime stores during the period.

make-a-scene.description: Interactive funnel deployed at Artefact to learn how to build a structured prompt and generate AI images, step by step.
make-a-scene.descriptionPublic:
**A tool to learn how to talk to an AI.**
Rather than reading guidelines, participants build a prompt word by word: image type, subject, action, location, style, lighting, ratio. Each choice is added in real time to the prompt bar visible at the bottom of the screen.

**A visible result at every step.**
Hovering over each option shows an example image to help choose. You only advance when all fields are filled, which forces prompt precision.

**Four images generated in seconds.**
Once the funnel is complete, the tool generates four visual variants from the built prompt. The full prompt is displayed and copyable. You can go back to any step and start over.

**Available in French and English.**
Bilingual interface, built for international teams.
make-a-scene.intro: Built internally at **Artefact**, Make a Scene is a **prompt engineering** training tool applied to image generation. Rather than reading guidelines, participants build a prompt word by word through a guided funnel: **3 steps, 14 sub-steps**. Each option has a preview image on hover. At the end of the funnel, 4 variants are generated in a single request. Available in English and French.

sliderSets.title: The funnel step by step
sliderSets.description: Each screen guides the user toward a keyword to select — image type, location, render style, ratio. The prompt assembles at the bottom of the screen as choices are made. The forward arrow only appears when all fields are filled.

sliderSets.title: From prompt to image
sliderSets.description: Once the funnel is complete, 4 variants are generated. A progress bar displays during generation, then the 4 images appear side by side, with the full prompt below, copyable with one click.

technologies.detail: App structured in distinct routes, each with its own dedicated layout. Generated images are stored in client-side memory and displayed without going through a backend.

A single request generates all 4 final variants server-side. No Promise.all, no polling: the Server Action waits for the complete response and returns it to the client in one block. The funnel preview images are generated once in an offline script and served statically, with no API calls at runtime.
A single store manages the entire funnel state: current step, selected values at each sub-step, gallery navigation flag, generated image URLs. No prop drilling across 3 steps × 14 sub-steps.
All design system components are developed and validated in isolation before being integrated. This guarantees visual consistency and makes it easier to get client feedback on each UI building block.
Interface available in English and French. All funnel questions, options, and labels are externalized in translation files, with a visible language selector.
Embla for option carousels and the results gallery. Radix UI Slider for the 3 advanced parameters in the final step, with native accessible rendering.

challenges.titre: 3-step × 14-sub-step funnel with back navigation
challenges.solution: The funnel is divided into 3 thematic steps, themselves split into 14 sub-steps. Each block of the assembled prompt is clickable: the user can navigate back directly to any sub-step without losing their choices. The entire funnel state is centralized in a Zustand store, which eliminates prop drilling and makes back navigation trivial to implement.

challenges.titre: Generating 4 images in a single request
challenges.solution: Rather than chaining multiple calls, a single request with the `n: 4` parameter generates all 4 variants server-side at once. No Promise.all, no polling: the Server Action waits for the complete response and returns it to the client. Result: simple code, minimal delay, no intermediate state management.

challenges.titre: Hover preview images without API calls
challenges.solution: Each funnel option has an example image visible on hover. Rather than generating these images on the fly, they were produced once in an offline script and are served statically. No API call at runtime, no latency for the user.

1 request, 4 variants via `n: 4` server-side
Single store · 3 × 14 sub-steps · no prop drilling
Design system components validated in isolation
FR / EN · externalized questions and options
Option carousels + accessible sliders

section01.subtitle: 3 steps, 14 sub-steps. Each chosen option is added in real time to the prompt bar at the bottom of the screen. At the end of the funnel, 4 image variants are generated in a single request.

bald-artiste.description: E-commerce site for Bald, an abstract painter: filterable gallery by collection, size, and price, artwork pages with sliders, online purchasing via Stripe, FR/EN internationalization.
bald-artiste.tagline: A *custom visual identity*, a *filterable gallery*, and *international* payments.
bald-artiste.descriptionPublic:
A fully custom visual identity. Every graphic element was designed for Bald: the logo split into two typefaces (BA in ink, LD in gold leaf), an animated circular badge, brushstrokes that animate as you scroll. This kind of visual impact can't be improvised with a template.

A gallery built for easy browsing. Visitors filter paintings by collection, dimensions, and price. What they're looking for is one click away, without any page reload.

Buy a painting in a few clicks. Stripe payment is integrated directly into the site. Bald can add or remove artworks from his admin panel, without touching the code.

The site in French and English. Every page, every artwork sheet, and every legal notice is available in both languages. An English-speaking visitor automatically lands on the right version.

The site is live at [bald-art.com](https://bald-art.com) — feel free to visit and see for yourself.
bald-artiste.intro: E-commerce site built freelance for Bald, an abstract painter. Black and gold visual identity built from scratch: animated intro, rotating circular badge, brushstrokes on scroll, Bebas Neue typography. Gallery with multi-criteria filters, complete Stripe checkout, and native FR/EN internationalization. The kind of site you can't build with a template.

sliderSets.title: The visual identity
sliderSets.description: Every graphic element (the logo, the rotating circular badge, the brushstrokes on scroll) was custom designed and integrated. Black and gold, Bebas Neue typography, GSAP animations: the entire BALD universe is built from scratch.

sliderSets.title: The filtered gallery
sliderSets.description: The gallery lets visitors filter artworks by collection and dimensions, and sort by ascending or descending price. With several dozen paintings, filtering is instant client-side (no reload, no network call).

technologies.detail: Full purchase checkout via Stripe Checkout for payment security and PCI compliance. No banking data handled server-side: the customer is redirected to Stripe, which handles the payment form, and a webhook signals confirmation to the site to mark the painting as sold.

The site showcases artworks — the visual rendering had to be smooth and refined. Lenis for soft, inertial scrolling, GSAP for scroll animations and page transitions, Motion (the evolution of Framer Motion) for more localized interface animations.

The site is available in French and English. i18n routing is handled by next-intl with separate translation files (every page, label, and error message is externalized). Language choice is persisted in the URL for SEO.

Gallery filters (collection, dimensions, price sorting) are stored in the URL via nuqs rather than in local React state. Direct consequence: a visitor can share the link to a filtered search, or refresh the page without losing their choices. No prop drilling, no global state.

Gallery pages and artwork sheets are statically generated for maximum performance and good organic search rankings. Mutations (purchase, stock management on the admin side) go through Server Actions, which revalidate the relevant pages after modification.

challenges.titre: Filterable gallery with multi-criteria sorting and shareable URL
challenges.solution: Artworks can be filtered by collection, dimensions, and sorted by price. With several dozen paintings, filtering had to remain instant client-side. The logic is encapsulated in a useMemo to avoid recalculation on every render, and the filter state is synced with the URL via nuqs: a visitor can copy-paste the link to a filtered view and find the exact same result on another device.

challenges.titre: Complete FR/EN internationalization
challenges.solution: All site content (gallery, artwork pages, legal pages, contact form) is available in French and English via next-intl. Language choice is persisted in the URL, and each page has its localized version for SEO. Visitors can switch languages at any time from the header selector.

challenges.titre: E-commerce legal pages, written for international art
challenges.solution: An online store comes with legal obligations: terms and conditions, privacy policy, return policy. These pages were written and integrated with the specifics of international art commerce (international shipping, customs rules, withdrawal period, mentions for unique works). Foundational work that's less visible but essential for a retail site to go live confidently.

russian-with-julia.titre: Website for an international Russian teacher
russian-with-julia.description: Freelance site for Julia, a Russian teacher with 500+ students in 25 countries: polished showcase, call booking in a few clicks, paid video course platform as an alternative to Podia or Teachable.
russian-with-julia.tagline: An *open-to-all* showcase, a private space for her students, and booking in *under a minute*.
russian-with-julia.descriptionPublic:
The site combines two worlds usually kept separate: a public showcase browsable with zero friction, and a private space for students who purchase video courses. All the marketing is open: you browse the sections, read the reviews, look at the world map of students, and can book a first call without creating an account.

The login only appears when it actually makes sense — to access already-purchased courses. The student receives a login link by email, no password, and accesses their dashboard. The teacher, meanwhile, has an admin area where she manages her courses, students, and calendar with full autonomy.

The site is live at [russianwithjulia.com](https://russianwithjulia.com) — feel free to visit and see for yourself.
russian-with-julia.intro: Complete freelance site for Julia, a private Russian teacher with ~~over 500 students~~ across ~~25 countries~~. The project's defining challenge: combining in one site a public-facing showcase, an online booking and payment system, and a paid video course platform purchasable per unit. Built so she could then be fully autonomous — she adds her courses, manages her students, and runs her site without needing me.

pillarsCards.titre: Stripe payment and Google Calendar chained
pillarsCards.description: The student picks a slot, pays online, receives the Google Calendar invitation with video call link: the entire chain completes in seconds, with no manual action from the teacher.

A real alternative to Podia or Teachable, with no recurring subscription and better protection than YouTube. Lesson packs purchasable per unit, videos served via signed URL from Cloudflare R2, zero egress fees.

Logo, palette, world map of students, bilingual testimonials, refined micro-interactions. An effective one-page home for marketing, a dedicated space for paid courses: a site worth browsing, that naturally drives traffic.

sliderSets.title: The world map of students
sliderSets.description: The world map is one of the sections I most enjoyed building. Each dot represents a real student: the map visually conveys the international dimension of Julia's teaching, reaching ~~over 500 people~~ across ~~25 countries~~. The most representative city names are positioned by hand, giving a more human feel than automatic placement. To see it live, the best thing is to visit [russianwithjulia.com](https://russianwithjulia.com).

sliderSets.title: Booking a first call
sliderSets.description: The booking widget takes up the right half of the screen: a calendar, available slots, a payment button, and that's it. The student picks their time, pays online, and immediately receives a Google Calendar invitation with a ready-to-use video call link. The event appears directly in the teacher's calendar, in her timezone. No manual entry, no email to send.

sliderSets.title: Bilingual testimonials, student space and admin
sliderSets.description: The review carousel scrolls continuously, mixing Cyrillic and Latin alphabets (I standardized the reviews in English). The component originally comes from [21st.dev](https://21st.dev), reworked to match the site's identity. On the student side, a dashboard gives access to purchased packs and streamed lessons. On the admin side, Julia creates her packages, uploads videos directly to storage, tracks her students, and connects her Google calendar.

challenges.titre: Combining a showcase site and authenticated app in one
challenges.solution: Most sites do one or the other: a presentation one-pager, or a real application requiring login from the start. Here both coexist. All marketing stays public, instantly accessible. The login only appears when it genuinely makes sense (buying a pack or accessing already-purchased courses). No authentication wall on first contact, but a real secure session for the paid section.

challenges.titre: Passwordless authentication (magic link)
challenges.solution: Both students and the teacher sign in by receiving a one-time link to their email: one click, and the session opens. No password to create, memorize, forget, or reset. Technically, Convex Auth handles the flow, with Resend for sending emails from a verified professional domain. Going live required configuring specific DNS records (SPF, DKIM) on the domain zone to ensure emails land in the inbox and not in spam.

challenges.titre: Payment and calendar chained in under five seconds
challenges.solution: The student picks a slot and pays via Stripe. Once the payment is confirmed server-side, a Google Calendar event is automatically created in the teacher's calendar, with a video call link generated on the fly and the student invited. The entire chain (payment, database write, event creation, invitation send) completes in a matter of seconds. The teacher has absolutely nothing to do between the booking and the appointment itself.

challenges.titre: Private video hosting on Cloudflare R2
challenges.solution: Cloudflare R2 was chosen over AWS S3 for one simple reason: R2 doesn't charge for outbound bandwidth, whereas S3 charges per gigabyte served. On video streaming, that's the cost that spikes first. The 10 GB free tier is more than enough for language courses, and even beyond that, costs remain minimal. Above all, it's a real alternative to Podia or Teachable subscriptions at €20-40/month that had to be avoided. The admin uploads files directly from their browser via a pre-signed URL: the video never transits through the Next.js server, avoiding proxy costs and speeding up uploads.

challenges.titre: World map of students: manual placement over algorithm
challenges.solution: The map is rendered by [dotted-map](https://www.npmjs.com/package/dotted-map), a small library that transforms a world map into a styled dot grid. First attempt at placing city names: an automatic algorithm to avoid label overlaps, with dotted lines connecting each name to its dot. Too fragile, and the result looked robotic. Switched to a manual system: in developer mode, an overlay lets you place each label with the mouse, positions are stored locally, and a button exports the JSON to paste into the code for production. Human result, adjustable in two minutes when a new country is added.

challenges.titre: Full client ownership of her tools
challenges.solution: The domain, the Stripe payment account, the Resend email account, Google Cloud access: everything is opened in the teacher's name, not the developer's. My access is limited to temporary credentials shared via video call while configuring services. If she changes developers tomorrow, everything stays in place and belongs to her (no migration needed). This logic is documented in the project source code as ADRs (Architecture Decision Records), reusable as a template for future freelance projects.

projet.citation: I wanted a site that carries the silence of a cellar and the light of a harvest morning. Léo listened, then he wrote.

piliers.texte: Each limited-edition cuvée is reserved before bottling. Real-time stock counter, numbered certificate generated at payment, custom label on order.

piliers.titre:
Guided tours
bookable in one step.
piliers.texte: Three tasting formats, a calendar that respects the moon and the harvest, payment at booking, Google Calendar invitation with directions attached.

piliers.titre:
Five suites, five
stories to inhabit.
piliers.texte: A multi-resource calendar that orchestrates rooms, the restaurant, and the tasting room together. A single booking can compose an entire stay.

challenges.titre: Multi-resource calendar
challenges.extrait: A room, the tasting room, and the table can be booked together or separately. The business logic forbids certain combinations (tasting room only on Saturdays, suite + table coupled in high season) and conflicts are verified at the Convex transaction level.

challenges.titre: Live stock for limited editions
challenges.extrait: Each cuvée has a reserved / sold / remaining counter that updates in real time via Convex subscriptions. Zero risk of double-selling on the last bottles of a 200-unit cuvée.

challenges.titre: Sanity + ISR for the journal
challenges.extrait: The winemaker writes in Sanity Studio. The Next.js front regenerates editorial pages via on-demand revalidation: a new post is live in under ten seconds, without a full rebuild.

challenges.titre: Animated SVG estate map
challenges.extrait: Rather than a heavy Mapbox integration, I drew the estate and its plots by hand in SVG. The path reveals on scroll, the climates light up on hover. 12 KB gzip, zero blocking JS.

challenges.titre: Three languages, one source
challenges.extrait: FR, EN and Japanese via next-intl. Sanity content is localized field by field; static pages are generated per locale at build time so each version has its own clean URL and SEO.

sectionII.subtitle: The estate isn't a single business but a trilogy: the cellar, the tasting room, the guest house. Each world required its own distinct approach, without ever losing coherence.
